Title: Verify that a valid frontend JWT token is generated upon a successful request with an empty JSON body.
Description: Verify that a valid frontend JWT token is generated upon a successful request with an empty JSON body.
Steps:
1. Send a POST request to "/api/auth/frontend-token".
2. Set "Content-Type: application/json".
3. Send an empty JSON body: {}.
Expected Result:
Status code 200 OK.
Response body contains a non-empty string representing the JWT token.
The token is a valid JWT format (e.g., header.payload.signature).

Title: Verify the API handles requests with a missing body gracefully.
Description: Verify the API handles requests with a missing body gracefully.
Steps:
1. Send a POST request to "/api/auth/frontend-token".
2. Set "Content-Type: application/json".
3. Do not send any request body.
Expected Result:
Status code 400 Bad Request or 422 Unprocessable Entity.
Response body contains a clear error message indicating a missing or invalid request body.

Title: Verify the API rejects requests with malformed JSON in the body.
Description: Verify the API rejects requests with malformed JSON in the body.
Steps:
1. Send a POST request to "/api/auth/frontend-token".
2. Set "Content-Type: application/json".
3. Send a malformed JSON body (e.g., {"key": "value").
Expected Result:
Status code 400 Bad Request.
Response body contains a clear error message indicating malformed JSON.

Title: Verify the API rejects requests using unsupported HTTP methods.
Description: Verify the API rejects requests using unsupported HTTP methods.
Steps:
1. Attempt to send a GET request to "/api/auth/frontend-token".
2. Attempt to send a PUT request to "/api/auth/frontend-token".
3. Attempt to send a DELETE request to "/api/auth/frontend-token".
Expected Result:
Status code 405 Method Not Allowed for all unsupported methods.
Response headers may include "Allow: POST".

Title: Verify the API rejects requests with an unsupported Content-Type header.
Description: Verify the API rejects requests with an unsupported Content-Type header.
Steps:
1. Send a POST request to "/api/auth/frontend-token".
2. Set "Content-Type: text/plain".
3. Send an empty body or a plain text string.
Expected Result:
Status code 415 Unsupported Media Type or 400 Bad Request.
Response body contains an error message indicating an unsupported content type.

Title: Verify the API handles requests with unexpected fields in the JSON body.
Description: Verify the API handles requests with unexpected fields in the JSON body.
Steps:
1. Send a POST request to "/api/auth/frontend-token".
2. Set "Content-Type: application/json".
3. Send a JSON body with an extra, unexpected field: {"extra_field": "some_value"}.
Expected Result:
Ideally, status code 400 Bad Request with an error message indicating unexpected fields.
Alternatively, status code 200 OK if the API is designed to ignore extra fields (less secure, but common). If 200, ensure the token is still generated correctly.

Title: Verify the format of the generated JWT token is correct and adheres to JWT standards.
Description: Verify the format of the generated JWT token is correct and adheres to JWT standards.
Precondition: A successful token generation request (APIMSDAT-001) has been executed.
Steps:
1. Perform a successful token generation request.
2. Extract the generated token from the response.
3. Validate that the token consists of three parts separated by dots (.).
4. Validate that each part is a base64url encoded string.
Expected Result:
The token string matches the header.payload.signature format.
Each segment can be successfully base64url decoded.

Title: Verify the generated JWT token contains a valid future expiration timestamp.
Description: Verify the generated JWT token contains a valid future expiration timestamp.
Precondition: A successful token generation request (APIMSDAT-001) has been executed.
Steps:
1. Perform a successful token generation request.
2. Extract the generated token.
3. Decode the token's payload (second part) to retrieve claims.
4. Verify the presence of the "exp" (expiration time) claim.
5. Verify that the "exp" timestamp is in the future relative to the current time.
Expected Result:
The decoded token payload contains an "exp" claim.
The "exp" value is a valid Unix timestamp.
The "exp" timestamp is greater than the current time, ensuring the token is valid upon generation.

Title: Verify the API implements rate limiting to prevent abuse and resource exhaustion from repeated requests.
Description: Verify the API implements rate limiting to prevent abuse and resource exhaustion from repeated requests.
Steps:
1. Send a large number of successful token generation requests (e.g., 100 requests) in a short period.
2. Monitor response times and status codes.
Expected Result:
After a certain threshold, subsequent requests should return a 429 Too Many Requests status code.
Response headers should include "X-RateLimit-Limit", "X-RateLimit-Remaining", and "X-RateLimit-Reset".
The API should remain responsive and not crash under load.

Title: Verify the API's CORS policy is correctly configured to prevent unauthorized cross-origin requests.
Description: Verify the API's CORS policy is correctly configured to prevent unauthorized cross-origin requests.
Steps:
1. Send an OPTIONS preflight request to "/api/auth/frontend-token" from an unauthorized origin (e.g., "http://malicious-site.com").
2. Send a POST request to "/api/auth/frontend-token" with an "Origin" header set to an unauthorized domain.
Expected Result:
For unauthorized origins, the "Access-Control-Allow-Origin" header should not include the unauthorized origin, or should be absent.
The API should reject the request (e.g., 403 Forbidden or no "Access-Control-Allow-Origin" header allowing the request).

Title: Verify that error responses do not disclose sensitive server-side information (e.g., stack traces, internal IP addresses, specific library versions).
Description: Verify that error responses do not disclose sensitive server-side information (e.g., stack traces, internal IP addresses, specific library versions).
Steps:
1. Trigger various error conditions (e.g., Malformed JSON, Invalid HTTP Method, Missing Body, Invalid Content-Type).
2. Examine the content of the error responses.
Expected Result:
Error responses should be generic and user-friendly.
No stack traces, database errors, internal server details, or specific technology versions should be exposed.
Error messages should be consistent in format (e.g., JSON with "code" and "message" fields).

Title: Verify that providing authentication headers to this endpoint (which doesn't require them) does not lead to unexpected behavior or errors.
Description: Verify that providing authentication headers to this endpoint (which doesn't require them) does not lead to unexpected behavior or errors.
Steps:
1. Send a POST request to "/api/auth/frontend-token" with an empty JSON body.
2. Include an "Authorization" header with a dummy JWT (e.g., Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0NTY3ODkwIiwibmFtZSI6IkpvaG4gRG9lIiwiaWF0IjoxNTE2MjM5MDIyfQ.SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c).
3. Include an "X-Frontend-JWT" header with a dummy JWT.
Test Data: Request Body: {}, Headers: Authorization: Bearer <dummy_jwt>, X-Frontend-JWT: <dummy_jwt>
Expected Result:
Status code 200 OK.
A valid frontend JWT token is still generated, indicating the endpoint correctly ignores irrelevant authentication headers for its operation.
No errors or unexpected behavior occur due to the presence of these headers.
